summary: The cell membrane is composed of a phospholipid bilayer with integral and peripheral proteins.
The cell membrane is composed of a cholesterol bilayer with only integral proteins.
The cell membrane is a rigid structure made entirely of carbohydrates.
The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is approximately -70 mV, maintained by the sodium-potassium ATPase pump.
The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is approximately -40 mV, maintained by calcium channels.
The resting membrane potential is +70 mV and requires no energy to maintain.
Osmosis is the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane from low solute concentration to high solute concentration.
Osmosis is the movement of solute across a semipermeable membrane from high to low concentration.
Osmosis requires ATP and is a form of active transport.
Active transport requires ATP and moves substances against their concentration gradient.
Active transport moves substances along their concentration gradient without energy.
Active transport uses osmotic pressure to move water molecules only.
Intracellular fluid constitutes approximately two-thirds of total body water, and extracellular fluid constitutes one-third.
Extracellular fluid constitutes two-thirds of total body water, and intracellular fluid constitutes one-third.
Intracellular and extracellular fluids are equal in volume.
The sodium-potassium ATPase pump moves 3 Na+ ions out of the cell and 2 K+ ions into the cell per cycle.
The sodium-potassium ATPase pump moves 2 Na+ ions out and 3 K+ ions in per cycle.
The sodium-potassium ATPase pump moves equal numbers of Na+ and K+ ions.
A hypertonic solution causes cells to shrink (crenation), while a hypotonic solution causes cells to swell and potentially lyse.
A hypertonic solution causes cells to swell, while a hypotonic solution causes cells to shrink.
Isotonic solutions always cause haemolysis of red blood cells.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the fundamentals of cell physiology.
Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
